item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The note hidden in the files was unexpected, nice touch. Well hidden like the text in 231.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article could not be sourced, and thus it is to be assumed it is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit. That may have crossed the line into kneejerk accusation. Still unsure what this exactly entails and why the sandbox isn't suitable though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's for an experiment, all of this will be cleared tomorrow

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why exactly are we using mainlist SCPs to host hidden shitty memes and blurbs again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edits still permitted, but please keep them short

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not a realistically breakable cipher. At a bare minimum, spaces to determine word length would be necessary, and it should be at least twice as long so that we can see how each letter is used in different contexts.

A few automated online cipher solvers couldn't make anything of it. Of course, if it's not a cipher, then we've really got nothing AFAIK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have a 33 character string containing what I count as 22 separate symbols, only one of which is paired, and there's no context for translating the phrase, so (assuming this is only alphabetic) you've got 1.69*10^25 possible letter substitutions. I have my doubts anybody will be able to decode this. If you gave us a paragraph of text with actual spaces, maybe…

What the hell, a random stab:

  • "Σ" appears as a double letter, most likely a consonant. Let's go with L.
  • "3KV" appears twice, and "3" appears 4 times. I'm going to make a guess that 3KV is ERS

#ϟ0+9ER< <QVERS.MELLFϟ7UF+8"98ERS

…yeah, not so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
brain just decided to quit

This is in the questionable area of multiverse beings. Brains don't Just decide to quit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess their eyes couldn't see past 24 FPS. Peasants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really supposed to be something everyone decodes, it's more of a challenge to anyone who likes to decode things like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon reflection I think I'm gonna neutral vote as what others have said is true. There's parts of this I like but there is a lot going on with weak links to each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the use of the calculator is somewhat interesting, the experiments are ultimately without rhyme or reason.

Experiment SCP-2618-02
Subject: D-030482-38
Procedure: Typed '34' into SCP-2618 and hit activation button while pointing SCP-2618 across the hall.
Result: D-030482-38 appeared in SCP-███'s holding cell, approximately 34 meters along the line SCP-2618 had been aimed. Both SCP-2618-1 and D-030482-38 were safely recovered.

Experiments like these scream of truly horrendous protocols. Since it is not the first time doing this (and they can interrogate the user for answers), why use "34" when there are much smaller integers to work with? Why not conduct it in a controlled area (like a desert plain)? Why not have precautions, like guards stationed near the estimated arrival point or GPS tracking system?

#ϟ0+93K< <QV3KV.M3ΣΣFϟ7UF+8"983KV

You honestly can't expect the general reader to take their time to decode this. If the articles does not bother, why should the reader bother about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with Kal on this one, lots of stuff going on and I don't find much of it particularly satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one seems kind of flung against the wall. There's a bunch of stuff about multiverses, a bunch of detailed tests on a thing that does a thing, a pitiable person in containment that's more a bundle of seeming neuroses than a character as such.

The article seems unfocused and clunky as I read it; there's a lot of different elements floating around, but nothing that really drives a clear narrative. The Series III tendency toward overexplanation and layering on of addenda is very much in evidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yes, the code at the end is translatable

only hint I'm gonna give is that the hastag is a lie and there are no spaces
